title: GLN — Global Location Number
objectives: Distinguish legal, physical and functional locations
Use GLNs correctly in EDI / EPCIS

sections.markdown: A **GLN** (Global Location Number) is a 13-digit identifier for a party or place — a company, a warehouse, a store, a hospital ward, a specific dock door. It's how supply chain messages (EDI orders, EPCIS events, invoices) address 'where' or 'who' with a single global key.

rows: A warehouse, store, dock door, ward

sections.code:
GLN structure (13 digits):
  GCP + Location Reference + Check Digit
Same check-digit algorithm as GTIN.

Usage in EDI / EPCIS:
  bizLocation:  urn:epc:id:sgln:8901234.00021.0
  readPoint:    urn:epc:id:sgln:8901234.00021.dock5
  (Extension after '.' identifies the sub-location.)

sections.markdown: One GLN can act as multiple parties: same location as 'ship-to,' 'bill-to,' 'end user.' In EDI (EDIFACT / X12) and EPCIS, GLNs replace free-text addresses — the whole supply chain agrees on the same key, so integrations don't break on name changes.

Using one GLN for many distinct physical sites. If two warehouses share the same GLN, you can't route inventory correctly. Issue distinct GLNs per physical location, plus GLN extensions for dock doors / rooms / shelves.

flashcards.back: 13-digit global identifier for a party (company/department) or a place (warehouse/store/dock/ward).
A suffix identifying a sub-location within a GLN (e.g., dock5 in an SGLN).
Same algorithm — mod-10 with weight-3-and-1 pattern.

quiz.explain: Locations or parties in the supply chain.
One physical location = one GLN; sub-locations via extensions.
SGLN identifies exactly where the event was captured.

challenges.text: Compute a GLN check digit; verify against a known correct GLN.
Model warehouse + dock doors as GLN + extensions in your DB.
Emit an EPCIS `readPoint` and `bizLocation` from a mobile scan event.
Design GLN allocation policy for a retailer with 500 stores and multiple functional GLNs each.
